name: Queenstown
tagline: Adventure capital of the world — with more family fun than you'd expect
summary: Queenstown is famous for extreme adventure, but it's also one of NZ's best family destinations. The gondola and luge alone are worth the trip, the scenery is jaw-dropping, and there's enough to fill a week without spending a fortune — if you plan smart.
verdict: Best NZ destination for families with kids aged 5+. Expensive, but the experiences are world-class.
best_for: Families with kids 5 and up, especially those who love the outdoors
not_ideal_for: Toddlers or families on a tight budget
free_activities: Queenstown Gardens and frisbee golf
Lake Wakatipu foreshore walk
Frankton Beach
Fernhill Track (easy walk with lake views)
Queenstown Hill Time Walk (older kids)
rainy_day: Skyline Gondola (enclosed gondola + restaurant)
Queenstown Ice Arena
MINT Games + Activities (Queenstown CBD)
Movie at Reading Cinemas Queenstown

day1.title: Arrival + Gondola Day
activities: Arrive and check in
Skyline Gondola & Luge (afternoon)
Walk the Queenstown foreshore
Dinner at Fergburger (iconic, queues are real — go at 5pm)

day2.title: Lake & Adventure Day
activities: TSS Earnslaw Steamship cruise (morning)
Picnic at Frankton Beach (free)
Jet boat experience on the Kawarau or Dart River
Ice cream on The Mall

day3.title: Day Trip to Arrowtown or Wanaka
activities: Drive to Arrowtown (20 mins) — free gold rush historic town
Lakes District Museum (kids love the gold panning)
Drive back via Lake Hayes (iconic photo stop)
Or swap for Wanaka: Puzzling World + lake swim

budget_breakdown.transport_notes: Rental car recommended. Fuel: ~$50/day if day-tripping.

faqs.q: Is Queenstown good for toddlers?
faqs.a: It's manageable but not ideal. The luge has a minimum height requirement, jet boats have minimum ages, and most adventure activities are for 5+. The gondola, foreshore, and Frankton Beach work great for under-5s.

faqs.q: What is the cheapest time to visit Queenstown?
faqs.a: April–May (autumn) is shoulder season with lower prices and stunning foliage. June–August (winter) is ski season — expensive. July–August is the peak-price period for everything.

faqs.q: Do I need to book Queenstown activities in advance?
faqs.a: Yes, especially in December–January. The Skyline and jet boats book out weeks ahead in peak season. Use Bookme for discounts.

seo_keywords: things to do queenstown with children
hidden_gems: Kelvin Heights Peninsula loop walk (free, 45 min, stunning lake views, almost no tourists)
Remarkables Market every Saturday summer — fresh food, local crafts, kids love the atmosphere
Jack's Point walking track — dramatic views of The Remarkables, free, rarely mentioned in guides
Arrowtown's Chinese Settlement is free and fascinating — one of the best-preserved Chinese settlements in NZ
Lake Hayes walkway (4km loop, completely flat, great for young kids, free parking at Estate Rd)
Kawarau Bridge bungee is free to watch — kids love watching others jump from the viewing platform
Pick cherries at Central Otago orchards in January (Cromwell, 45 min away) — $5–8/kg direct from the orchard

name: Rotorua
tagline: Geothermal wonder, Maori culture, and more activities per square km than anywhere in NZ
summary: Rotorua is arguably NZ's best pure family destination. It's affordable, centrally located, packed with activities, and the geothermal parks are genuinely unlike anything kids will have experienced. Budget around $250–400/day for a family of four.
verdict: Our #1 pick for first-time NZ family visitors. High activity density, affordable, and unforgettable.
best_for: All ages — genuinely excellent for 2-year-olds through teenagers
not_ideal_for: Those sensitive to the sulphur smell (it fades after a day)
free_activities: Government Gardens and croquet lawn
Lake Rotorua foreshore walk
Kuirau Park (free public geothermal park — bubbling mud!)
Sulphur Point Scenic Reserve
Redwood Forest (free walking tracks)
rainy_day: Te Puia (most areas covered)
Agrodome (all indoors)
Polynesian Spa (thermal pools — great in rain)
Rotorua Museum (when open)

day1.title: Geothermal + Culture
activities: Te Puia morning (geysers + kiwi house + Maori arts centre)
Lunch in town
Kuirau Park (free bubbling mud pools)
Polynesian Spa at sunset

day2.title: Farm, Gondola & Zorbing
activities: Agrodome morning farm show
Skyline Gondola & Luge (afternoon)
OGO zorbing (book ahead)
Burger Wisconsin for dinner

day3.title: Wai-O-Tapu + Rainbow Springs
activities: Wai-O-Tapu thermal wonderland (30 mins south, 9am Lady Knox geyser)
Rainbow Springs Nature Park (kiwi, trout, native birds)
Redwood Forest walk (free, magical)
Depart or relax

budget_breakdown.transport_notes: Rental car or campervan. Most sites within 30 mins. Fuel: ~$20/day.

faqs.q: Does Rotorua smell bad?
faqs.a: Yes — the sulphur (rotten egg) smell is real, especially near geothermal areas. Most visitors stop noticing after a few hours. Kids often find it funny. It's not harmful.

faqs.q: How many days do you need in Rotorua?
faqs.a: 2–3 days covers the highlights. 4 days if you want to do day trips to Wai-O-Tapu and the Hobbiton Movie Set (45 mins away).

faqs.q: Is Hobbiton worth it with kids?
faqs.a: Older kids (8+) who know Lord of the Rings love it. Under-8s may not get much from it. At $49/adult it's a significant cost — prioritise other activities for younger kids.

seo_keywords: best things to do rotorua with children
hidden_gems: Kuirau Park — free geothermal park right in Rotorua. Hot bubbling mud pools, steam vents, and a foot spa. Completely free and often empty of tourists
Rainbow Springs just outside town has a free kiwi viewing at dusk if you buy the daytime ticket ($42 adult) — includes nocturnal house with real kiwis
Government Gardens rose beds and croquet lawns are free and beautiful — locals picnic here while tourists pay to enter Te Puia across the road
Redwood Forest free walking trails — world-class trails through giant coastal redwoods. Free, 10 mins from town
Lake Rotorua foreshore at sunset — completely free, geothermal steam rising off the lake, otherworldly atmosphere
Eat at Atticus Finch or Pig & Whistle rather than tourist strip on Fenton St — same quality, 30% cheaper
Book Wai-O-Tapu early morning (it opens at 8:30am) — Lady Knox Geyser performs at 10:15am sharp and is included in entry

facebook_groups.description: Local Rotorua families sharing tips, activity deals and holiday advice

facebook_groups.description: Tips from NZ families on making the most of your North Island trip

name: Taupo
tagline: Lake Taupo, Huka Falls, and a relaxed pace — perfect mid-island stop
summary: Taupo sits at the centre of the North Island and makes the perfect stopping point on any road trip. The Huka Falls are spectacular and free, Lake Taupo is huge and swimmable, and the town has enough to keep a family busy for 2–3 days without blowing the budget.
verdict: An essential road trip stop. Best as 2 nights between Rotorua and Wellington.
best_for: All ages, especially great for a relaxed lake holiday
not_ideal_for: Those wanting non-stop activities (it's a chill destination)
free_activities: Huka Falls viewing platform and walkway
Lake Taupo swimming (Acacia Bay, Wharewaka Point)
Spa Thermal Park (free outdoor hot springs)
Taupo Harbour foreshore
Aratiatia Rapids (controlled release 10am, 12pm, 2pm, 4pm in summer)
rainy_day: AC Baths thermal pools
Wairakei Terraces indoor pools
Taupo Museum
Bowling at Taupo Tenpin

day1.title: Huka Falls & Lake Swim
activities: Huka Falls walk (free, 20 mins from town)
Aratiatia Rapids (watch the controlled release)
Swim at Acacia Bay
Spa Thermal Park sunset soak (free)

day2.title: Jet Boat & Town Day
activities: Huka Jet boat experience
Taupo Museum
Walk the Taupo Harbour foreshore
AC Baths thermal pools (afternoon)

day3.title: Departure or Day Trip
activities: Wairakei Terraces morning
Drive south to Tongariro (2h) for dramatic volcanic scenery
Or head north back to Rotorua

budget_breakdown.transport_notes: Easy to explore by car. Free parking most attractions.

faqs.q: Can you swim in Lake Taupo?
faqs.a: Yes — Acacia Bay and Wharewaka Point are the best family swimming spots. Clean, clear water. Cold outside of December–February.

faqs.q: Is the Spa Thermal Park really free?
faqs.a: Yes. It's a natural hot spring on the Waikato River bank, accessed via a walking track. No facilities, no fee. Bring a towel.

faqs.q: How far is Taupo from Rotorua?
faqs.a: About 1 hour by car. Most families combine both into one trip.

seo_keywords: things to do taupo with children
hidden_gems: Taupo Bungy spectator area is free — kids love watching the bungee jumpers launch out over the Waikato River
Huka Prawn Park has a FREE prawn feeding area — you only pay if you want to fish
Acacia Bay is Taupo's best-kept secret — calm swimming, sandy shore, no tourists, 10 min from town centre
Walk the Huka Falls from upstream — start at Wairakei Park, walk 1km to the falls. Completely free, incredible turquoise water views
Craters of the Moon (geothermal walk): adults $10, children free. Less crowded than Te Puia and still spectacular
Taupo Night Market runs Thursday evenings in summer — $5 hangi ($20 for family of 4), live music, craft stalls
Wairakei Terraces has a FREE hot stream walking path along the edge of the property — steam, pools, no entry fee

facebook_groups.name: Lake Taupo Families
facebook_groups.description: Local advice on Taupo holidays, lake activities and hidden spots

name: Christchurch
tagline: Gateway to the South Island — and surprisingly great for families
summary: Christchurch is the South Island hub and a genuinely underrated family destination. The International Antarctic Centre is world-class, the Gondola has incredible views, and it's one of the most affordable major cities to visit in NZ. Plus it's the base for road trips to Queenstown, Kaikoura and the West Coast.
verdict: Underrated family city. 2 nights is ideal — great base for South Island road trips.
best_for: Families using it as a South Island base, or those wanting an affordable city break
not_ideal_for: Those wanting beach holidays (beaches are cold, not tropical)
free_activities: Botanic Gardens (stunning, free)
Hagley Park
Te Ana Ngāi Tahu Rock Art Centre (free Tue–Fri)
New Regent Street (restored heritage streetscape)
Avon River Precinct walk
rainy_day: International Antarctic Centre (fully indoor, outstanding)
Canterbury Museum (free)
Air Force Museum (free)
Timezone Christchurch

day1.title: Antarctic Centre + City
activities: International Antarctic Centre (spend 3+ hours)
New Regent Street lunch
Botanic Gardens walk (free)
Punting on the Avon (optional)

day2.title: Gondola + Wildlife
activities: Christchurch Gondola morning (views of Banks Peninsula and Alps)
Orana Wildlife Park (open range zoo, afternoon)
Dinner in Riccarton

day3.title: Lyttelton or Departure
activities: Drive to Lyttelton Harbour (20 mins)
Ferry to Diamond Harbour for a picnic
Lyttelton Farmers Market (Sat only)
Depart for Queenstown or Kaikoura

budget_breakdown.transport_notes: Car useful for Gondola and Orana Park. City centre walkable.

faqs.q: Is Christchurch worth visiting after the earthquakes?
faqs.a: Absolutely. The rebuild has created one of NZ's most interesting city centres — street art, container malls, modern architecture alongside heritage buildings. The 'gap filler' culture is unique.

faqs.q: What is Christchurch best known for?
faqs.a: The Antarctic Centre (genuinely world-class), the Gondola, and as the South Island's main hub. Also known for its garden city parks and punting on the Avon River.

hidden_gems: Canterbury Museum is completely FREE — world-class natural history and Antarctic exhibitions. Kids love the Antarctic section with real gear
Christchurch Art Gallery is free — rotating exhibitions, kids' programmes, excellent café
Willowbank Wildlife Reserve in Harewood has the most accessible kiwi viewing in NZ — see kiwis walking around in a semi-dark enclosure at close range ($30 adult, $15 child)
Hagley Park has a free outdoor splash pad and playground near the Antigua Boat Sheds
Riccarton House and Bush — free walk through native bush in the middle of the city, tui everywhere
Christchurch's free tram now runs again — take it to see the earthquake rebuild story
Lane's Emporium in Little High Eatery — 8 food vendors under one roof, $8–15 mains, great for fussy eaters

name: Bay of Islands
tagline: 144 islands, dolphins, sailing, and New Zealand's birthplace — all in one bay
summary: The Bay of Islands is NZ's original holiday destination and still one of the best. Paihia is the main family base — from there you can swim with dolphins, sail to deserted islands, visit the Waitangi Treaty Grounds, and enjoy some of the warmest water in the country.
verdict: Best NZ beach holiday for families. Come in January for the warmest water. Combine with the Kauri Coast.
best_for: Beach-loving families, water activities, NZ history
not_ideal_for: Those wanting to avoid the 3.5-hour drive from Auckland

top_activities.name: Hole in the Rock (Cape Brett cruise)
Island Beach Picnic (sailing to a private island)

free_activities: Paihia Beach swim
Waitangi Beach (calm and protected)
Haruru Falls walk (2.5km return)
Russell foreshore walk (take the $15 ferry)
Te Ti Bay mangrove boardwalk
rainy_day: Waitangi Treaty Grounds (indoor museum component)
Paihia bowling or mini golf
Drive to Kerikeri and visit the Stone Store (oldest stone building in NZ)

day1.title: Arrival + Waitangi
activities: Arrive Paihia
Waitangi Treaty Grounds (afternoon)
Paihia Beach swim
Fish and chips on the wharf for dinner

day2.title: Dolphin Cruise + Islands
activities: Morning dolphin watching cruise (book in advance)
Russell ferry trip ($15 return) + lunch at historic Duke of Marlborough Hotel
Afternoon beach time

day3.title: Hole in the Rock or Departure
activities: Full-day Hole in the Rock cruise via Cape Brett
Or Haruru Falls walk + Kerikeri exploration
Depart for Auckland

budget_breakdown.transport_notes: Car needed to get there. Once in Paihia, everything is walkable or boat-based.

faqs.q: Can you actually swim with dolphins at the Bay of Islands?
faqs.a: Yes, but it depends on the dolphins cooperating. Most operators run swim/watch tours. Guaranteed sightings but not guaranteed in-water encounters. Book with Explore NZ or Fullers.

faqs.q: How far is Bay of Islands from Auckland?
faqs.a: About 3.5 hours by car via SH1. Most families drive up — no direct train. Flights take 40 mins to Kerikeri.

faqs.q: Is Russell worth visiting?
faqs.a: Yes — take the 10-minute ferry from Paihia ($15 return). Russell is NZ's first European settlement, quiet and historic. Great for lunch.

seo_keywords: bay of islands family holiday nz
hidden_gems: Haruru Falls — a horseshoe waterfall 10 min from Paihia. Free, easy 20-min walk from the car park, completely off most tourist itineraries
Waitangi Mangrove boardwalk — free 1km boardwalk through the mangroves behind the Treaty Grounds. Kids love spotting fish and mud crabs
Russell is 15 min by ferry from Paihia ($8 return) — quieter, historic, better cafes, gorgeous beaches (Long Beach is a 10-min walk from town)
Urupukapuka Island day trips — DOC-run island with ancient Maori pa sites and stunning beaches. Ferry from Paihia $45 adult, $23 child return
Poor Knights Islands glass-bottom boat: you don't need to scuba dive — the glass-bottom tours show the underwater world for all ages from Tutukaka
Paihia Night Market (summer Fridays) — fresh seafood, live music, $5–10 snacks, very family-friendly
Cape Brett lighthouse walk (experienced hikers only) — 15km return, takes all day, rewards with dramatic coastal views and a possible guided hut overnight

name: Wanaka
tagline: Queenstown's quieter, more beautiful neighbour — and better for families
summary: Wanaka is what Queenstown was before the crowds arrived. The lake is stunning, the town is relaxed, Puzzling World is a quirky family classic, and Treble Cone offers winter skiing for those with older kids. In summer, it's a beach town with mountains.
verdict: Better than Queenstown for a relaxed family holiday. Pair both together on a South Island trip.
best_for: Families who want scenery without Queenstown prices and crowds
not_ideal_for: Those who want maximum activity options (fewer than Queenstown)
free_activities: Wanaka Lakeshore walk (the most photographed lake in NZ)
Mt Iron track (1.5h return, views over Wanaka)
Glendhu Bay (swim beach, 10 mins from town)
Outlet Track (flat family walk along the Clutha River)
rainy_day: Puzzling World (fully indoor)
Cinema Paradiso (legendary small cinema — BYO food)
Wanaka Beerworks tour

day1.title: Lake Day
activities: Drive in via the Crown Range (spectacular road)
Wanaka lakeshore and That Wanaka Tree photo stop
Paddleboard or kayak rental
Dinner at Ritual Coffee (best burgers in town)

day2.title: Puzzling World + Mt Iron
activities: Puzzling World morning (2+ hours)
Picnic at Glendhu Bay
Mt Iron track (views)
Cinema Paradiso evening (legendary experience)

day3.title: Queenstown Day Trip
activities: Day trip to Queenstown (1h drive)
Skyline Gondola & Luge
Return to Wanaka for dinner

budget_breakdown.transport_notes: Car essential. Crown Range road is spectacular but steep — take it slow.

faqs.q: Is Wanaka better than Queenstown for families?
faqs.a: For young children (under 8) or families wanting a relaxed pace, yes. Wanaka is calmer, cheaper, and the lake is more accessible. For older kids wanting adventure activities, Queenstown has more options.

faqs.q: Is the Crown Range road safe to drive?
faqs.a: Yes, in good weather. It's NZ's highest paved road and spectacular. Avoid in icy conditions (winter/early spring). The alternative via Cromwell is longer but easier.

seo_keywords: things to do wanaka with children
hidden_gems: Diamond Lake and Rocky Mountain track — a 3-hour loop starting 20 min from Wanaka. Stunning views of Lake Wanaka and the Matukituki Valley. Suitable for kids 7+
Wanaka's famous lone willow tree is best at sunset or early morning — avoid midday when it's packed with photographers
Mount Iron track (45 min return from near town) — a volcanic dome with 360-degree views. Kids 6+ can handle it
Glendhu Bay — 12km from Wanaka, a DOC campsite with one of the best lake swimming spots in the South Island. $10/person/night to stay overnight
Wanaka Beerworks has a child-friendly tap room with non-alcoholic options — a local hangout that's actually relaxed for families
Stuart Landsborough's Puzzling World: buy the combined illusion room + maze ticket ($18 adult, $13 child). The maze alone takes 45+ minutes — seriously tricky
Cardona ski field road (don't ski) — drive up the Cardona Valley road in summer for jaw-dropping scenery and alpine meadow walks

facebook_groups.name: Wanaka Families
facebook_groups.description: Local Wanaka tips, lake activities and family holiday advice

facebook_groups.name: Wanaka and Queenstown Families
facebook_groups.description: Tips covering both destinations — the most popular South Island family pairing

name: Wellington
tagline: Te Papa, the coolest little capital in the world, and NZ's best café culture
summary: Wellington punches well above its weight as a family destination. Te Papa is the best free museum in NZ, the cable car is a Wellington institution, and the waterfront is brilliant for kids. It's also NZ's windiest city — plan for indoor backup options.
verdict: Best NZ city break for families — 2–3 days is perfect. Affordable, compact, world-class museum.
best_for: Families who love culture, museums, and great food
not_ideal_for: Beach holidays — Wellington's beaches are cold and windy
free_activities: Te Papa Museum (permanent collection)
Wellington Waterfront and Frank Kitts Park playground
Botanical Garden (top of cable car — walk back down through the garden)
Weta Workshop Unleashed — exterior is free to walk around
Mount Victoria Lookout (drive up for 360° city views)
hidden_gems: Kāpiti Island day trips — pre-book DOC permit ($16/adult, $8/child). One of NZ's best conservation successes, teeming with native birds including kiwi
Makara Beach — 30 min from the CBD, wild west coast, free, almost no tourists. Great rock pooling with kids
Wellington Night Market (Friday, Chaffers Street) — incredible street food from $5, very family-friendly
Te Papa's basement Discovery Centres are often overlooked — free interactive zones specifically for kids under 12
The Weta Cave (free entry) — Weta Workshop's free exhibition. The paid tour ($45/adult) is brilliant for movie-loving kids 8+
Otari-Wilton's Bush — NZ's only public native botanic garden. Free, beautiful, and usually empty
Interislander Ferry (Picton to Wellington) — even as a day trip, the Marlborough Sounds passage is spectacular. Book foot passenger return
rainy_day: Te Papa Museum — could spend a full day
Carter Observatory planetarium shows
Queensgate Mall (Lower Hutt) — large mall with cinema 20 min away
Weta Workshop Unleashed (immersive experience, covered)
Reading Cinemas Courtenay Place

day1.title: Te Papa + Waterfront
activities: Te Papa Museum (3–4 hours — don't rush)
Waterfront walk and Frank Kitts Park
Cuban Street lunch ($12–15 mains)
Wellington Cable Car up and walk back through Botanical Garden

day2.title: Zealandia + City
activities: Zealandia Wildlife Sanctuary (book first entry)
Weta Cave (free)
Mount Victoria Lookout (drive up)
Courtenay Place for dinner — lots of family options

day3.title: Day Trip Option
activities: Option A: Drive to the Wairarapa (1h) — Martinborough wine village, free beaches at Cape Palliser
Option B: Interislander ferry day trip to Picton through Marlborough Sounds

budget_breakdown.transport_notes: Compact CBD — walk or use GO Wellington buses. Cable car $12 return.

faqs.q: Is Wellington worth visiting with kids?
faqs.a: Absolutely. Te Papa alone justifies the trip — it's free, world-class, and kids can spend 3–4 hours there. Add the cable car, Zealandia, and the waterfront and you have a brilliant 2–3 day family break.

faqs.q: How windy is Wellington really?
faqs.a: Very. It earns its reputation. Always pack a windproof jacket. The wind doesn't stop you doing anything but it makes outdoor activities less pleasant in winter. Summer winds are actually refreshing in the heat.

faqs.q: Can you do Wellington as a stopover between North and South Island?
faqs.a: Yes — this is a common itinerary. Fly or drive to Wellington, spend 2 nights, then take the Interislander ferry to Picton for the South Island leg.

name: Nelson & Abel Tasman
tagline: NZ's sunniest region — golden beaches, clear water, and the most beautiful coastal walk in the world
summary: Nelson is the sunniest city in New Zealand and the gateway to Abel Tasman National Park — a coastline of golden sand beaches, crystal-clear water, and native bush. It's also one of the most relaxed and family-friendly regions in the country.
verdict: Criminally underrated by international visitors. NZ families know it — the Abel Tasman coast is spectacular and the region is brilliant for all ages.
best_for: Beach lovers, kayakers, and families who want stunning scenery without big crowds
not_ideal_for: Families wanting theme parks or urban entertainment

top_activities.name: Abel Tasman Water Taxi + Beach Day

free_activities: Tahunanui Beach — Nelson's main beach, safe swimming, playground, holiday park
Abel Tasman coastal track sections (walk in from road ends for free)
Nelson's Centre of New Zealand hill walk (20 min return, 360° views)
Rabbit Island Recreation Reserve — beautiful beach and bush
Miyazu Japanese Garden (free, peaceful)
hidden_gems: Tōtaranui Beach (Abel Tasman northern end) — the most spectacular beach in the park, accessible by road (gravel), DOC campsite on-site. Book campsites months ahead
Kaiteriteri Beach — the main Abel Tasman gateway beach is free to access and one of the best family beaches in NZ. Arrive early in summer
Mapua Wharf — 30 min from Nelson, boutique food village on the waterfront. Ice cream, fish and chips, gallery. Best evening out in the region
Golden Bay (over Takaka Hill) — worth the winding road for Pohara Beach and the spectacular Farewell Spit. Far fewer tourists
Wai-iti Lavender Farm (summer only) — free to walk around, kids love the bees and the colour
Nelson Saturday Market at Montgomery Square — the best market in NZ's South Island. Local produce, crafts, hot food, always buzzing
rainy_day: Nelson Provincial Museum (free, excellent NZ history)
World of WearableArt museum (Nelson) — $24/adult, $12/child
Founders Heritage Park with vintage train rides
Nelson's café strip on Trafalgar Street

day1.title: Nelson City + Tahunanui Beach
activities: Arrive Nelson, settle in
Tahunanui Beach afternoon swim
Nelson Saturday Market if weekend
Walk up Centre of New Zealand at sunset

day2.title: Abel Tasman Day
activities: Water taxi to Anchorage or Bark Bay beach
Swim, snorkel, beach lunch
Water taxi back
Mapua Wharf for dinner

day3.title: Golden Bay or Kayaking
activities: Option A: Drive over Takaka Hill to Golden Bay (Pohara Beach)
Option B: Half-day guided sea kayak in Abel Tasman

budget_breakdown.transport_notes: Rental car essential. Water taxis for Abel Tasman from Kaiteriteri or Marahau.

faqs.q: Do you need to walk the full Abel Tasman track?
faqs.a: No — the water taxi is the best family option. You hop on, get dropped at a beach, swim and explore, then get picked up later. No multi-day walking required.

faqs.q: Is Nelson or Kaiteriteri a better base?
faqs.a: Nelson for more amenities and a broader area; Kaiteriteri for direct Abel Tasman access. Most families base in Nelson and day-trip to Kaiteriteri (50 min drive).

faqs.q: When is Abel Tasman best for families?
faqs.a: December to March. The water is warmest (18–20°C) and all water taxis and kayak operators are fully running. October and April are quieter and still good.

facebook_groups.name: Nelson Families NZ
facebook_groups.description: Local Nelson families sharing tips, events and holiday recommendations

facebook_groups.name: Abel Tasman NZ Visitors
facebook_groups.description: Trip reports and advice for Abel Tasman National Park visitors

name: Coromandel
tagline: Hot Water Beach, Cathedral Cove, and some of NZ's most beautiful beaches within 2.5 hours of Auckland
summary: The Coromandel Peninsula is Auckland's backyard holiday spot — and for good reason. Hot Water Beach (dig your own hot pool in the sand), Cathedral Cove sea arch, Thames goldfields, and a string of gorgeous beaches make it one of the best NZ family destinations. Best in summer.
verdict: Essential for Auckland families and any North Island road trip. Hot Water Beach alone makes it worth visiting.
best_for: Beach holidays, Auckland day trips or overnights, hot spring lovers
not_ideal_for: Families wanting theme parks or rainy-day activities — it's very outdoor-focused
free_activities: Hahei Beach — one of NZ's most beautiful beaches, free
Cathedral Cove walk (35 min each way from car park)
Whitianga waterfront and beach
Thames Estuary and Dickson Holiday Park beach walk
Cook's Beach — safe swimming, sheltered
hidden_gems: Hot Water Beach at dawn — arrive 90 mins before low tide on a weekday morning. You'll have it almost to yourself vs the chaotic peak-time crowds
Opito Bay — one of the most stunning beaches on the peninsula, hard to find, rarely crowded. Locals go here instead of Hahei
Te Whanganui-A-Hei Marine Reserve snorkelling near Cathedral Cove — some of the best snorkelling in NZ, completely free
Coromandel town (the actual town) — often skipped as people go straight to Hahei. The Coromandel Smoking Company smoked fish and chips are legendary ($15 a serve)
Karangahake Gorge windows walk (Thames side) — goes through old gold mine tunnels with a torch. Free, very cool for kids
Long Bay near Whitianga — far less visited than Hahei, beautiful swimming, rarely mentioned in guides
rainy_day: Driving Creek Railway (covered train ride through sculpture park)
Gold Mine Experience Thames
Thames Historical Museum
Miranda Shorebird Centre (45 min from Thames)

where_to_stay.name: Hot Water Beach TOP 10
family_features: 5 min walk to Hot Water Beach

day1.title: Arrive + Cathedral Cove
activities: Drive from Auckland (2h 30m)
Cathedral Cove — walk or boat (check tides)
Hahei Beach afternoon swim
Fish and chips at Hahei

day2.title: Hot Water Beach
activities: Hot Water Beach at low tide — check hotwatertide.co.nz the night before
Hire spades ($5) and dig your hot pool
Afternoon at Hahei or Cook's Beach
Drive to Whitianga for dinner

day3.title: Driving Creek + Drive Home
activities: Driving Creek Railway (Coromandel town, 9am booking)
Lunch at Coromandel Smoking Company
Drive back to Auckland via Thames

budget_breakdown.transport_notes: Car essential. Fuel up in Thames — petrol is expensive on the peninsula.

faqs.q: When is Hot Water Beach best?
faqs.a: The geothermal seep is only accessible 2 hours either side of low tide. Check hotwatertide.co.nz before you go. Weekday mornings in shoulder season are the least crowded.

faqs.q: Is Cathedral Cove better by boat or on foot?
faqs.a: Boat if you have young kids (avoids the 35-min walk each way). On foot if your kids can manage it — the walk is lovely. The sea arch looks the same either way.

faqs.q: Can you do Coromandel as a day trip from Auckland?
faqs.a: Yes — just about. Hot Water Beach + Cathedral Cove is a very long day (5h driving total). Better to stay 1–2 nights and actually enjoy it.

facebook_groups.name: Coromandel Families NZ
facebook_groups.description: Trip reports, accommodation tips and activity advice for the Coromandel Peninsula

facebook_groups.name: Auckland Day Trips with Kids
facebook_groups.description: Auckland families sharing day trip and weekend getaway ideas

name: Fiordland & Milford Sound
tagline: The most dramatic scenery in New Zealand — Milford Sound will stop your kids mid-sentence
summary: Fiordland is remote, dramatic, and genuinely unlike anywhere else on Earth. Milford Sound — sheer 1,500m peaks rising from dark water, waterfalls cascading year-round, dolphins surfing the bow wave of your cruise boat — is a once-in-a-lifetime experience. The drive in from Te Anau is as spectacular as the destination.
verdict: Requires a detour and some planning, but Milford Sound is the single most dramatic landscape experience in NZ. Worth every bit of the effort.
best_for: Families with kids 5+ who appreciate epic scenery. Teenagers are consistently blown away.
not_ideal_for: Families needing lots of activities or beach time. Milford Sound itself has limited things to do beyond the cruise.
free_activities: The Milford Sound highway drive itself (SH94) — one of the world's great drives
Mirror Lakes stop (10 min from Te Anau) — perfectly still reflections, boardwalk
Lake Te Anau waterfront and lakeside walk
The Chasm (free, 10-min walk, dramatic gorge — kids love it)
Monkey Creek and Homer Tunnel viewpoints on SH94
hidden_gems: Cruise in the rain — Milford Sound is famous for waterfalls. Rain creates hundreds of extra waterfalls cascading down the cliffs. A wet day is genuinely the best time to cruise.
Book the first cruise of the day (earliest departure) — before the tour buses from Queenstown arrive, Milford has a completely different atmosphere
The Chasm on SH94 is often rushed past — the 10-min return walk to the churning gorge is spectacular and completely free
Te Anau town is the best base — stay here rather than driving to Milford and back from Queenstown in one day (brutal 8h round trip)
Mirror Lakes at golden hour (late afternoon) — the reflection only works on calm days and the light is magical in the evening
Fiordland Cinema in Te Anau plays 'Ata Whenua — Shadowland' (30 min film about Fiordland) for $15 — excellent context before your Milford cruise
rainy_day: Milford Sound cruise (rain = more waterfalls = better experience)
Te Anau Glowworm Caves (underground, weather irrelevant)
Fiordland Cinema (Te Anau)
Te Anau cafes and the waterfront walk

day1.title: Queenstown to Te Anau
activities: Drive Queenstown to Te Anau (2h via SH6)
Lake Te Anau waterfront walk
Te Anau Glowworm Caves (book ahead)
Dinner at The Fat Duck or Sandfly Café

day2.title: Milford Sound Cruise Day
activities: Early start — drive SH94 to Milford (2h, stop at Mirror Lakes and The Chasm)
Morning Milford Sound cruise (book earliest available)
Explore Milford after cruise
Drive back to Te Anau

day3.title: Te Anau + Depart
activities: Kepler Track day section (Lake Te Anau lookout, 3–4h return for fit families)
Or relaxed morning in Te Anau
Drive to Queenstown (2h) or Dunedin (4h 30m)

budget_breakdown.transport_notes: Own car essential — no public transport to Milford Sound. Fill up with fuel in Te Anau.

faqs.q: Is Milford Sound better from Queenstown or Te Anau?
faqs.a: Always base in Te Anau. Driving from Queenstown is 4h each way — an exhausting 8h round trip that leaves little time at Milford itself. Te Anau is 2h from Milford and a lovely base.

faqs.q: What's the best Milford Sound cruise?
faqs.a: Real Journeys and Cruise Milford are the main reputable operators. The 2-hour cruise covers the full fiord. The 'nature cruise' with commentary is better for families than the cheaper scenic cruises.

faqs.q: Is the drive to Milford Sound safe with kids?
faqs.a: Yes — SH94 is well-maintained and spectacular. Allow 2h from Te Anau with stops. The Homer Tunnel (1.2km, one lane, traffic lights) is exciting for kids. Check the NZ Transport Agency website for any closures.

facebook_groups.name: Fiordland and Milford Sound Visitors
facebook_groups.description: Trip reports and booking tips for Milford Sound cruises and Te Anau

facebook_groups.name: NZ South Island Families
facebook_groups.description: South Island road trips, holiday parks and family destinations

name: Northland
tagline: Cape Reinga, Ninety Mile Beach, and ancient kauri forests — NZ's subtropical north
summary: Northland is where New Zealand begins — Cape Reinga at the very tip, Ninety Mile Beach stretching impossibly far, the Bay of Islands (its own destination), and ancient kauri trees that were old when Rome fell. It is warm, uncrowded outside peak season, and full of both Maori history and natural spectacle.
verdict: NZ's most underrated family destination. Visitors rush past Northland to get to the Bay of Islands or Rotorua — which means the rest of Northland is blissfully uncrowded.
best_for: Families who love beach walks, history, and genuinely off-the-beaten-track NZ
not_ideal_for: Families who need packed activity schedules — Northland rewards slow travel

top_activities.name: Cape Reinga Lighthouse
top_activities.price_note: Free — walking track to the tip

free_activities: Cape Reinga walkway — 30-min return to the very tip of NZ (where the Tasman Sea meets the Pacific Ocean)
Ninety Mile Beach driving (4WD only at most points, but free to walk and watch)
Mangawhai Heads beach walk and estuary
Waipoua Forest — Tane Mahuta (Lord of the Forest kauri tree) is free to walk to
Rawene village and Hokianga Harbour views
Opononi and Omapere beaches on Hokianga Harbour
hidden_gems: Matai Bay (Karikari Peninsula) — two beaches facing different directions, protected water perfect for kids, barely signposted
Tokerau Beach on Karikari Peninsula — one of NZ's finest beaches, almost deserted outside New Year
Wenderholm Regional Park — first regional park south of Whangarei, beautiful beach and pohutukawa trees
Pukenui wharf — a humble fishing wharf where locals catch snapper right off the dock, kids can watch or try their luck
Matauri Bay — stunning beach where the Rainbow Warrior wreck sits offshore (visible by snorkel)
Waipu Caves — glowworm cave you can self-explore with a torch, $5 entry, virtually unknown
rainy_day: Kauri Museum in Matakohe — world's best kauri collection, genuinely fascinating
Whangarei Museum and Native Bird Recovery Centre
The Hundertwasser Art Centre in Whangarei — unique architecture, free entry
Whangarei Town Basin — covered walkway, cafes, and the clock museum
Explore Kerikeri — charming town, great cafes, Stone Store (NZ's oldest stone building)

day_1.title: Auckland to Northland — Kauri Forest and Hokianga
activities: Drive from Auckland north (2.5h to Waipoua)
Tane Mahuta walkway — see the largest living kauri tree in NZ (free, 10-min walk)
Hokianga Harbour — ferry to Omapere sand dunes
Night at Rawene or Opononi

day_2.title: Far North — Cape Reinga and Ninety Mile Beach
activities: Drive north to Kaitaia (1h 30m from Hokianga)
Optional: join a Cape Reinga day tour (includes Ninety Mile Beach sandboarding, $85-120/person)
Cape Reinga lighthouse walk — 30 minutes return, the spiritual departure point of Maori souls
Te Paki Stream — body boards down the stream outlet to the beach (bring your own or hire)

day_3.title: Doubtless Bay and Karikari Peninsula
activities: Matai Bay — double beach, protected water, perfect for young swimmers
Mangonui fish and chip shop — one of NZ's most famous fish and chip shops, eat on the wharf
Drive south back to Auckland or continue to Bay of Islands

budget_breakdown.food_per_day: $60-120 (great fish and chips, seafood direct from source)
budget_breakdown.transport_notes: 4-5h from Auckland, fuel is the main cost. Some Northland activities (Cape Reinga tours) include transport.

faqs.q: Is Cape Reinga worth the drive with kids?
faqs.a: Yes, genuinely — but it is a long day from Paihia (4h return driving) or an even longer day from Auckland. The tip of NZ where two oceans meet is a genuinely moving experience, and the Ninety Mile Beach sandboarding on the way back is a family highlight. Join an organised day tour rather than self-driving — the tour takes the beach route which is only accessible to experienced 4WD drivers.

faqs.q: What is the best base for a Northland family holiday?
faqs.a: Paihia (Bay of Islands) works for the southern Northland attractions. For the Far North (Cape Reinga, Doubtless Bay), base at Kaitaia or Mangonui. For Hokianga and Waipoua Forest, base at Opononi. A 4-5 day Northland loop starting and ending in Auckland covers all the main areas.

facebook_groups.name: Northland NZ Family Travel
facebook_groups.description: Families sharing Northland trip tips, accommodation reviews, and hidden beaches

facebook_groups.name: NZ Camping and Holiday Parks
facebook_groups.description: Holiday park recommendations across Northland and NZ

name: Tauranga / Mount Maunganui
tagline: NZ's most popular beach town — Mount Beach, surf, and the best fish and chips on the coast
summary: Tauranga and Mount Maunganui ('the Mount') is where New Zealand families have been going for summer holidays for generations. Mount Beach is NZ's best family beach — gentle surf, lifeguards, warm water, and excellent town amenities. It is unpretentious, warm, and has everything a family needs within walking distance.
verdict: NZ's best all-round family beach town. Not as dramatic as the Coromandel or Bay of Islands, but consistently excellent for families who want reliable beach conditions and good amenities.
best_for: Beach holidays, families with kids who want to surf or bodyboard, relaxed summer holidays
not_ideal_for: Families who need cultural or activity-heavy itineraries — it is primarily a beach destination

quick_facts.nearest_airport: Tauranga (TRG) — Air NZ from Auckland daily. Or drive 2.5h from Auckland.

top_activities.name: Papamoa Beach
top_activities.price_note: Free — 22km beach, quieter than the Mount

free_activities: Mount Beach — NZ's best family surf beach, free lifeguards December through February
Walk to the summit of Mount Maunganui (232m, 1-1.5 hours return, panoramic Bay of Plenty views)
Papamoa Hills Regional Park — walks with views over the bay
Tauranga waterfront — flat walk along the harbour
Mount Maunganui main shopping street and cafes
Pilot Bay — calm harbour-side beach for toddlers, sheltered from surf
hidden_gems: Pilot Bay on the harbour side of the Mount — completely sheltered from surf, perfect for toddlers learning to swim, almost unknown to tourists
Omanu Beach (north of Mount Beach) — same sand, slightly less crowded, popular with local families
The Strand in Tauranga — the city's main waterfront, good for an evening walk with excellent cafes
McLaren Falls Park — 20 mins south of Tauranga, beautiful waterfall and lake, free, rarely visited by tourists
Waihi Beach — 40 mins north, beautiful and quieter than the Mount with the same sand quality
Katikati Bird Gardens — small but excellent, rarely on tourist itineraries
rainy_day: The Hot Pools at the Mount — heated indoor pools, great for a wet day
Tauranga Historic Village — open-air museum with working heritage buildings
Bayfair Shopping Centre — NZ's best regional mall
Rotorua (1h away) — thermal parks operate in all weather
Waimarino Adventure Park — kayaking and water activities (partially weather-dependent)

day_1.title: Arrive and Hit the Beach
activities: Check in and walk to Mount Beach
Bodyboard or swim — bodyboards for hire near the beach
Surf lesson booking for tomorrow
Fish and chips dinner on the waterfront — Little Big Tree or Harbourside is the local favourite

day_2.title: Surf Lesson and Mountain Summit
activities: Morning surf lesson (9am, 2 hours, board and wetsuit included)
Post-lesson: the Hot Pools to soak tired muscles
Afternoon: walk to the Mount summit — 1.5 hours return, stunning Bay of Plenty views
Evening: ice cream on Mount Maunganui main street

day_3.title: Day Trip — Rotorua or Waihi Beach
activities: Option A: Rotorua day trip (1h) — Wai-O-Tapu or Agrodome for a geothermal contrast
Option B: Waihi Beach day trip (40 mins) — quieter, beautiful beach, then the Waihi goldmine museum
Return to the Mount for final evening

budget_breakdown.activities_per_day: $60-180 (beach is free, surf lessons and hot pools are the main costs)
budget_breakdown.food_per_day: $80-140 (excellent cafes and fish and chip shops, self-catering easy from local supermarkets)
budget_breakdown.transport_notes: 2.5h from Auckland. $250-350 in fuel Auckland return. Most activities are walkable from accommodation.

faqs.q: Is Mount Maunganui beach safe for young children?
faqs.a: Mount Beach has lifeguards from December to February and is generally one of NZ's safer surf beaches for families. Pilot Bay on the harbour side of the Mount is completely sheltered and perfect for toddlers. Young children who cannot swim confidently should stick to Pilot Bay or the shallow shore break at Mount Beach between the flags.

faqs.q: How far is Tauranga from Rotorua?
faqs.a: Just under 1 hour (62km). This makes Tauranga an excellent beach base with easy day-trip access to Rotorua's geothermal parks and farm shows. A 5-day trip could combine 3 nights at the Mount (beach focus) with 2 nights in Rotorua.

facebook_groups.name: Mount Maunganui Family Holidays
facebook_groups.description: Families sharing tips for Mount Maunganui — accommodation, beach conditions, and activities

facebook_groups.name: Bay of Plenty NZ Family Travel
facebook_groups.description: Bay of Plenty families sharing local knowledge and trip recommendations

name: Hawke's Bay
tagline: Art Deco Napier, Cape Kidnappers gannets, and NZ's best fruit — a hidden family gem
summary: Hawke's Bay is one of New Zealand's most underrated family destinations. It has Napier — the world's most complete Art Deco city — alongside Cape Kidnappers (the world's most accessible mainland gannet colony), excellent beaches, and NZ's warmest, driest climate outside Marlborough. It is cheaper, quieter, and more relaxed than the famous tourist destinations.
verdict: Excellent value, genuinely unique (the Art Deco city and gannet colony are world-class), and consistently overlooked by tourists focused on Rotorua and Queenstown. Great for families who want something different.
best_for: Families who want unique experiences away from the main tourist circuit, ages 5+
not_ideal_for: Families wanting adrenaline activities or mountain scenery

quick_facts.nearest_airport: Hawke's Bay Airport (NPE) — Air NZ from Auckland and Wellington daily

free_activities: Napier Art Deco walking — the entire CBD is a living museum of 1930s architecture, free to walk
Marine Parade — Napier's famous waterfront with sunken gardens, free fountains, skate park
Waimarama Beach — 30 mins south of Napier, long black sand beach backed by cliffs
Pekapeka Wetlands — free bird sanctuary walk, black swans and waterfowl
Havelock North village — charming, excellent cafes, free to explore
Te Mata Peak — 30-min drive from Napier, 399m summit, panoramic Hawke's Bay views, free
hidden_gems: Cape Kidnapper tractor trek at low tide (only accessible this way, more dramatic than the walking track) — book ahead with Gannet Beach Adventures
Matahiwi Marae near Hastings — authentic Maori marae with open visits, call ahead
Fruit stalls along Omahu Road — direct from orchards, half the supermarket price in season
Ahuriri suburb in Napier — waterfront dining and cafes in a converted industrial area, much cheaper than the main Art Deco strip
Black Reef at Napier — snorkelling spot known to locals, excellent clear water on calm days
Dinosaur Museum in Hastings — small but excellent, very reasonably priced
rainy_day: Hawke's Bay Museum and Art Gallery in Napier — free, excellent Art Deco earthquake exhibition
Napier Aquarium — small but good for young kids
Hawke's Bay Opera House — check for shows and events
Indoor swimming at Splash Planet Hastings — water park, best in rain
Hastings City Library — good children's section, free

day_1.title: Napier Art Deco and Waterfront
activities: Art Deco walk through Napier CBD (self-guided, free — pick up a map from i-SITE)
Marine Parade waterfront walk — sunken gardens, fountains, and Ocean Spa
National Aquarium of New Zealand (small but good for under-10s)
Dinner in Ahuriri suburb — better value than the tourist strip

day_2.title: Cape Kidnappers — Gannet Colony
activities: Cape Kidnappers tractor tour (book well ahead) — 10,000+ gannets nesting at the world's most accessible mainland colony
Return to Napier via Te Mata Peak (30-min detour, stunning views)
Havelock North village for lunch
Afternoon: Waimarama Beach or Ocean Beach swimming

day_3.title: Orchards, Markets, and Wine
activities: Hastings Farmers' Market (Sunday, 8:30am-12:30pm) — outstanding fresh produce and local food
Strawberry or apple picking at an Omahu Road orchard (in season)
Explore Hastings and the twin city of Havelock North
Drive south towards Wellington or loop back to Taupo and Rotorua

budget_breakdown.activities_per_day: $60-200 (Cape Kidnappers tour is the main paid expense)
budget_breakdown.food_per_day: $70-130 (excellent local food direct from orchards, good cafes in Napier and Havelock North)
budget_breakdown.transport_notes: 4h from Auckland, 4.5h from Wellington. Napier itself is compact and walkable. Car essential for Cape Kidnappers and beaches.

faqs.q: Is Cape Kidnappers worth visiting with young kids?
faqs.a: Yes, especially the tractor trek. Seeing 10,000+ gannets at close range is genuinely extraordinary for kids of any age. The tractor tour is accessible for ages 5+ and takes about 3 hours return. The walk (6.5km each way) is better for families with older kids (10+) who can handle the distance. Book the tractor tour well in advance — it sells out frequently in summer.

faqs.q: What is the Art Deco connection in Napier?
faqs.a: Napier was devastated by a massive earthquake in 1931 and rebuilt almost entirely in the Art Deco architectural style popular at the time. The result is one of the world's most complete and intact Art Deco cities. The self-guided walk is free and takes 1-2 hours. Children who are not interested in architecture still enjoy the elaborate facades, the old-fashioned shop fronts, and the Marine Parade waterfront.

facebook_groups.name: Hawke's Bay Family Activities
facebook_groups.description: Local and visiting families sharing Hawke's Bay activity tips and recommendations

facebook_groups.name: NZ East Coast Family Travel
facebook_groups.description: Families sharing east coast NZ travel tips from Napier to Gisborne

name: Dunedin
tagline: Yellow-eyed penguins, albatross, sea lions, and NZ's most dramatic urban wildlife experience
summary: Dunedin is New Zealand's wildlife capital — the Otago Peninsula is the only place in the world where you can see royal albatross, yellow-eyed penguins, and New Zealand sea lions all within an hour of a city centre. It is also a beautiful Victorian city with excellent cafes, a world-class university, and the world's steepest street (Baldwin Street).
verdict: Outstanding for families who love wildlife. The Otago Peninsula wildlife experiences are genuinely world-class and unlike anything else in NZ. Worth a 2-3 day detour for any South Island road trip.
best_for: Families who love wildlife and nature, ages 5+ (best for 8+ for the wildlife tours)
not_ideal_for: Beach holidays or adventure activities — Dunedin is cold and grey much of the year

quick_facts.nearest_airport: Dunedin (DUD) — Air NZ from Auckland, Wellington, and Christchurch daily

top_activities.name: Royal Albatross Centre (Taiaroa Head)
top_activities.price_note: $60/adult, $20/child — guided tour to view nesting albatross

top_activities.name: Speight's Brewery tour
top_activities.price_note: $30/adult, free under 18 (guided tour, not tasting for kids)

free_activities: Baldwin Street — walk or run up the world's steepest street (1:2.86 gradient), take the photo
Dunedin Railway Station exterior — NZ's most photographed building, stunning Flemish Renaissance architecture
Otago Settlers Museum — free, excellent history of European and Maori settlement in Otago
Dunedin Botanic Garden — New Zealand's oldest botanic garden, free, with duck ponds and rhododendron garden
Otago Peninsula scenic drive — free to drive, stunning views even without stopping at paid attractions
Aramoana — 30-min drive, peaceful estuary and ocean beach
hidden_gems: Larnach Castle — NZ's only castle, 30 mins from Dunedin CBD, dramatic Gothic architecture, garden, and genuine ghost stories kids love ($30/adult)
Penguin Place (private conservation reserve) — the best yellow-eyed penguin viewing in NZ, more intimate than DOC reserves
Tunnel Beach — 30-minute walk to a hidden sea arch and tunnel carved by the sea (free), dramatically beautiful and almost unknown
Orokonui Ecosanctuary — predator-free sanctuary with kiwi, kaka, and tuatara — underrated alternative to Zealandia
Chinese Garden in the city centre — NZ's only authentic Chinese garden, free, unexpectedly beautiful
St Clair hot saltwater pool — outdoor ocean pool (heated), $6/adult, locals' secret
rainy_day: Otago Museum — excellent natural history and Pacific collections, free general entry
Toitu Otago Settlers Museum — New Zealand's best social history museum, free entry
Dunedin Public Art Gallery — free entry, good for older kids
University of Otago buildings — free to walk through the beautiful stone campus
Cadbury World (now closed but chocolate shops remain on Cumberland Street)

day_1.title: Dunedin City and Iconic Landmarks
activities: Baldwin Street — walk up the world's steepest street
Dunedin Railway Station — photo stop
Otago Settlers Museum (free) — 2 hours
Dunedin Botanic Garden walk
Octagon city centre for dinner

day_2.title: Otago Peninsula — Albatross and Penguins
activities: Morning: Royal Albatross Centre guided tour (10am or 11am — book ahead)
Lunch at the Portobello Hotel or packed lunch on the peninsula
Afternoon: Penguin Place or DOC penguin viewing (evening tour — penguins return from sea at dusk)
Look for sea lions on the rocks near Taiaroa Head (free to observe)

day_3.title: Larnach Castle and Tunnel Beach
activities: Larnach Castle (30 mins from city) — NZ's only castle, spectacular grounds, Gothic architecture
Tunnel Beach walk (free) — hidden sea arch and tunnel, 30-min walk each way
St Clair beach and hot saltwater pool
Drive south to Queenstown or north to Christchurch

budget_breakdown.activities_per_day: $80-200 (wildlife tours are the main expense, much of the city is free)
budget_breakdown.food_per_day: $80-150 (excellent cafes in the Octagon area, good fish and chip shops)
budget_breakdown.transport_notes: 3h from Queenstown, 3.5h from Christchurch. City is walkable for many attractions. Car needed for Otago Peninsula and Larnach Castle.

faqs.q: What age is suitable for the wildlife experiences in Dunedin?
faqs.a: The Royal Albatross Centre tour suits ages 5+ (some waiting in a hide). Yellow-eyed penguin viewing is best for ages 8+ as it involves waiting quietly at dusk — younger kids can find the stillness challenging. Sea lions are free to observe from distance and suit any age. All wildlife experiences require advance booking.

faqs.q: Is yellow-eyed penguin viewing worth the cost?
faqs.a: Yes — yellow-eyed penguins are one of the world's rarest penguins (approximately 4,000 remaining) and Dunedin is the most accessible place in the world to observe them. Penguin Place ($90/adult) provides the most intimate and conservation-focused experience. DOC viewing hides ($30/adult) are cheaper but less guided. Both are genuinely moving experiences that kids remember.

facebook_groups.description: Dunedin families sharing activities, wildlife tips, and recommendations for visitors with kids
